steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage sets up a contrast: scholars routinely characterize Larsen as representative of the Harlem Renaissance — the reasons may seem [blank] — but accepting that characterization risks overlooking subtleties in her style. The word in the blank must describe how the reasons appear on the surface: so solid and beyond question that scholars accept them without pushing further. The 'but' then signals those appearances are misleading.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'pretentious': means arrogant or showy; that describes the tone of the reasons, not whether they seem logically airtight. ✗
  • Choice B — 'incongruous': means out of place or inconsistent; if the reasons seemed incongruous, scholars would not accept them so readily. ✗
  • Choice C — 'irrefutable': means impossible to dispute; reasons that appear irrefutable would lead scholars to accept the characterization without questioning it — exactly the uncritical acceptance the 'but' clause warns against. ✓
  • Choice D — 'provocative': means controversial or stimulating debate; reasons that provoke debate would invite scrutiny, not suppress it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
